name: Idun
description: Idun is the Norse goddess associated with youth, spring, and immortality. She is the keeper of the magical apples that the Æsir gods must eat to maintain their eternal youth and vigor. Without Idun and her apples, the gods would age and weaken, making her a crucial figure in their continued existence and power. Idun is described as a beautiful and graceful goddess, often depicted with a basket or chest containing the golden apples. Her role extends beyond simply providing sustenance; she represents the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, much like the seasons she is connected to. The abduction of Idun by the giant Thjazi, tricked by Loki, is a pivotal event in Norse mythology, demonstrating her importance and the vulnerability of the gods without her. When Thjazi held Idun captive, the gods began to age rapidly. Loki, under pressure from the other gods, eventually rescued Idun, restoring their youthfulness. This tale highlights Idun's unique power and the dependence of the Æsir upon her for their immortality. She is also sometimes associated with poetry and inspiration, suggesting a broader connection to creativity and artistic expression, in addition to her vital role in maintaining the gods' youthful vitality.
subtitle: Goddess of Youth and Spring

keyMyths.title: Idun's Abduction by Thjazi
keyMyths.description: Loki, tricked by Thjazi, lured Idun away, leading to her capture and the gods' rapid aging. Loki eventually rescued her, returning the apples of youth. This highlights Idun's importance.

keyMyths.title: The Apples of Immortality
keyMyths.description: Idun's golden apples are the source of the gods' eternal youth and vigor. Without them, they would age and eventually die. They're a symbol of vitality.

keyMyths.title: Idun and Bragi
keyMyths.description: Idun is the wife of Bragi, the god of poetry and skalds. This association suggests a connection between youth, beauty, and creative inspiration. They are a powerful pair.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
